metadata.title: Rail Projects — Full Supply Chain Impact Analysis (Tiers 0–10)
metadata.description: This file contains a complete multi-tier, multi-database supply-chain impact assessment for three European rail projects drawn from the FAST-Infra labelling dataset.  Supply-chain tier convention: Tier 0 is the direct spend — a one-time transaction from the project to its immediate suppliers (construction, steel, energy, etc.) distributed via the SECTOR_ALLOC vector.  Tier 1 is the supply chain that arises from that direct spend: the suppliers of those suppliers, sourced from different sectors and different countries.  For each project the analysis covers: (a) tier 0 — the direct spend across 8 supplying sectors and its associated emissions, employment, water use, and value added; (b) tier 1 — the first supply-chain round with sourcing-country breakdown derived from the OECD TiVA bilateral trade structure; (c) tier 2 — second supply-chain round; and (d) tiers 3–10 — deeper upstream rounds, reported both per-tier and as an aggregate. All four calibrated MRIO databases are run in parallel so that cross-database uncertainty can be assessed directly from this file.

source_field_explanations.Project_ID: Unique alphanumeric identifier assigned to each rail project in the FAST-Infra pipeline. Format: <Asset class>_<Region>_<Stage>.
source_field_explanations.Region: Broad geographic region of the project. One of: Europe, LATAM, Africa, Asia. Used to select regional GHG/employment/water intensity multipliers in the IO model (see REGION_MULT in tvp_io_lib.py).
source_field_explanations.Stage: Project lifecycle stage. 'Dev' = development / capital expenditure phase (construction and commissioning); 'Op' = operational phase (energy, maintenance, and staffing expenditure). The stage determines which sector-allocation vector is used: Rail_Dev or Rail_Op.
source_field_explanations.Avoided_Emissions_Prior_Year_tCO2e: Greenhouse-gas emissions avoided in the prior year as a result of the project being operational, measured in tonnes of CO₂-equivalent (tCO2e). Scope: direct operational GHG avoidance only (e.g. modal shift from road to rail). Does NOT include supply-chain or embodied carbon. For Dev-stage projects this represents the projected annual avoidance once operational; for Op-stage projects it is the actual prior-year figure.
source_field_explanations.Pollution_Improvement_Type: Categorical description of the type of pollution reduction claimed by the project under the FAST-Infra E4 (Pollution & Waste) positive contribution criterion. 'Improved Air Quality' indicates a measurable reduction in particulate matter or NOx relative to a counterfactual transport mode. 'Not a claimed PC' means no formal pollution-reduction positive contribution is asserted for this project.
source_field_explanations.Pollution_Measure_CAPEX_Pct: Share of total CAPEX attributable to pollution-reduction measures as a proportion band (e.g. '<1%'). Used in the FAST-Infra scoring algorithm to weight the E4 positive-contribution score. Applies only when Pollution_Improvement_Type is not 'Not a claimed PC'.
source_field_explanations.People_Impacted_Pollution_Reduction: Number of people who benefit from the pollution-reduction measure. For the Dev project this is 150,000, reflecting the catchment population directly exposed to improved air quality along the rail corridor.
source_field_explanations.Resilience_Domain: FAST-Infra R5 (Resilience) domain claimed by the project. 'Critical infrastructure' means the project contributes to the resilience of a nationally or regionally significant infrastructure system (e.g. providing a low-carbon alternative to road freight that is less vulnerable to fuel price shocks). 'Not a claimed PC' means no formal resilience positive contribution is asserted.
source_field_explanations.Resilience_Measure_CAPEX_Pct: Share of total CAPEX attributable to resilience measures, as a proportion band. '<5%' for the Dev project reflects minor hardening and redundancy investments embedded in the civil works.
source_field_explanations.People_Impacted_Resilience: Annual number of people whose access to transport services is made more resilient by the project. 5,500,000 ppl/year for the Dev project is the projected annual ridership once operational.
source_field_explanations.Reach_Ppl_Yr: Total number of people with improved access to the rail service per year, as modelled in the FAST-Infra finance input file. This is the headline social reach indicator. N/A for operational-phase cost items which are accounting entries rather than separate projects.
source_field_explanations.PC_Air_Quality: Modelled positive-contribution score (0–1 scale) for air quality improvement under the FAST-Infra E4 criterion. Derived from the ratio of pollution-reduction CAPEX to total CAPEX, weighted by the number of people impacted. 0.008 for the Dev project reflects a small but genuine air-quality co-benefit relative to the total investment.
source_field_explanations.PC_Critical_Infra: Modelled positive-contribution score (0–1 scale) for critical infrastructure resilience under the FAST-Infra R5 criterion. 0.045 for the Dev project reflects the meaningful but not dominant resilience contribution of the project at national transport-system level.
source_field_explanations.Est_Capex_EUR: Estimated total capital expenditure in EUR. For the Dev project (€1,850,000,000) this is the full construction and commissioning cost of the rail line. For Op projects (€130,000 and €90,000) these are the annual operating expenditure figures modelled as a CAPEX-equivalent for supply chain tracing purposes.

eur_to_usd_conversion.source: European Central Bank (ECB) average EUR/USD exchange rate for 2023. ECB Statistical Data Warehouse, series EXR.D.USD.EUR.SP00.A. https://sdw.ecb.europa.eu. Applied uniformly to all three projects.

sector_code_assignment.Dev → Rail_Dev: Projects in the development (construction) phase use the 'Rail_Dev' sector-allocation vector: Construction 35%, Manufacturing 28%, Transport/Logistics 10%, Energy/Utilities 10%, Mining/Extraction 8%, Water/Waste 4%, Health/Social 4%, Agriculture 1%. This reflects the capital-goods-heavy spending profile of rail line construction — civil works, rolling stock procurement, signalling equipment, and electrification infrastructure. Source: ITF/OECD (2019); World Bank Railway Reform Toolkit (2021).
sector_code_assignment.Op → Rail_Op: Projects in the operational phase use the 'Rail_Op' sector-allocation vector: Energy/Utilities 35%, Transport/Logistics 20%, Manufacturing 15%, Health/Social 8%, Water/Waste 6%, Construction 10%, Mining/Extraction 5%, Agriculture 1%. This reflects the energy-dominated spending profile of running a railway — traction electricity, track maintenance, and administrative services. Source: ITF/OECD (2019).

exiobase.strengths: Widest environmental satellite coverage of any global MRIO: includes GHG by species (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, F-gases), water, land, materials, and air pollutants. Best choice for detailed environmental footprint work.
exiobase.limitations: Employment accounts less detailed than WIOD for services sectors. Water satellite uses withdrawal rather than consumption basis.
exiobase.citation: Stadler, K. et al. (2018). EXIOBASE 3: Developing a Time Series of Detailed Environmentally Extended Multi-Regional Input–Output Tables. Journal of Industrial Ecology 22(3):502–515. doi:10.1111/jiec.12715

eora26.strengths: Highest country resolution of any MRIO: 190 individual country tables. Best database for projects in Africa, LATAM, or smaller Asian economies where EXIOBASE and WIOD pool countries into RoW aggregates.
eora26.limitations: Only 26 sectors — coarser than EXIOBASE or OECD. Employment intensities are known to be upward-biased for developing-country sectors (documented in Lenzen et al. 2013). No dedicated water satellite account.
eora26.citation: Lenzen, M. et al. (2013). Building Eora: A Global Multi-Region Input–Output Database at High Country and Sector Resolution. Economic Systems Research 25(1):20–49. doi:10.1080/09535314.2012.761953

wiod.strengths: Best employment accounts for OECD countries: WIOD Socio-Economic Accounts (SEA) provide detailed compensation, hours, and employment by skill level. Time-series available 2000–2016.
wiod.limitations: No water satellite account (water impacts fall back to S_BASE global averages in this model). Older data vintage (2014) relative to EXIOBASE and OECD ICIO.
wiod.citation: Timmer, M.P. et al. (2015). An Illustrated User Guide to the World Input–Output Database: The Case of Global Automotive Production. Review of International Economics 23(3):575–605. doi:10.1111/roie.12178

oecd.strengths: Most up-to-date and methodologically consistent database for policy analysis. Fully aligned with OECD Trade in Value Added (TiVA) indicators. Best choice for value-added and international trade decomposition.
oecd.limitations: Fewer sectors (45) than EXIOBASE (163). Environmental satellite coverage limited compared to EXIOBASE.
oecd.citation: OECD (2021). OECD Inter-Country Input-Output Tables, 2021 edition. OECD Publishing, Paris. doi:10.1787/a8c8b9f0-en

GHG_tCO2e.description: Total greenhouse gas emissions attributed to the investment through the supply chain, expressed in tonnes of CO₂-equivalent using IPCC AR6 100-year global warming potentials (GWP100). Covers CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, and fluorinated gases (F-gases). At tier 0 this captures the direct production emissions of the goods and services purchased; at higher tiers it captures progressively deeper upstream extraction and manufacturing emissions.
GHG_tCO2e.interpretation: Higher values indicate more carbon-intensive supply chains. For rail projects, Manufacturing (steel, concrete) and Energy_Utilities drive most of the GHG at tiers 0–1. Tiers 3+ are dominated by Mining_Extraction (ore, coal, oil) and Agriculture. The sum across all tiers approximates the full embodied (Scope 3) carbon of the investment.
GHG_tCO2e.source: Intensity coefficients from EXIOBASE 3.8.1 satellite accounts, aggregated to 8 sectors (Stadler et al. 2018). Regional multipliers from IEA (2022) CO2 Emissions from Fuel Combustion and IPCC AR6 WG3 Annex II.

Employment_FTE.description: Number of full-time equivalent jobs supported throughout the supply chain by the investment. At tier 0 this is the employment directly generated by the supplying sectors (e.g. construction workers, steel mill workers). At higher tiers it captures jobs in upstream industries (quarrying, energy supply, transport). One FTE is defined as one person working full-time for one year (≈ 1,750–2,000 hours depending on national standards).
Employment_FTE.interpretation: Rail Dev projects in Europe generate the most jobs at tier 0 via Construction (FTE intensity: 14 per M$) and Health/Social administration services. The Africa multiplier (1.58×) means the same investment generates significantly more jobs if executed in sub-Saharan Africa, reflecting lower labour productivity and higher labour intensity of production.
Employment_FTE.source: Base intensities from EXIOBASE 3.8.1 employment satellite and ILO (2022) World Employment and Social Outlook. Regional multipliers from World Bank WDI (2022) labour productivity by sector.

Water_1000m3.scope: Supply-chain water withdrawal (blue water, direct + indirect)
Water_1000m3.description: Total freshwater withdrawn from surface water and groundwater sources by the supply chain to produce the goods and services purchased, expressed in thousands of cubic metres. Uses a water withdrawal (not consumption) basis — withdrawal includes water returned to the source after use. At tier 0 the water footprint is dominated by energy generation (cooling water) and manufacturing. Agriculture (5.0 × 10³ m³/M$) and Water_Waste (8.0 × 10³ m³/M$) sectors carry the highest intensity.
Water_1000m3.interpretation: For rail projects, water impact is relatively modest at tier 0 but grows at higher tiers as Agriculture and Water_Waste sectors gain weight in the upstream supply chain. Note that WIOD has no water satellite account so its water figures rely entirely on the global S_BASE averages.
Water_1000m3.source: Mekonnen & Hoekstra (2011) HESS 15:1577–1600; FAO AQUASTAT (2021); WRI Aqueduct (2019). Regional multipliers from Hoekstra et al. (2011) PNAS.

ValueAdded_M$.description: Total value added generated throughout the supply chain by the investment, expressed in millions of USD. Value added equals gross output minus intermediate inputs — it is equivalent to the GDP contribution of the supply chain activity. At tier 0 this approximates the direct economic multiplier: how many dollars of GDP are generated per dollar invested. Construction (VA ratio: 0.48) and Health_Social (0.70) have markedly different VA intensities, reflecting the capital-intensity gap between civil works and service delivery.
ValueAdded_M$.interpretation: A VA ratio > 1 is not possible at a single tier but cumulating across all tiers will approach the full Leontief multiplier. The value-added figures here are most reliable from the OECD database, which was specifically calibrated against OECD STAN national accounts.
ValueAdded_M$.source: OECD STAN (2022); World Bank WDI (2022). Value added ratios from EXIOBASE 3.8.1 value-added extensions.

tier_0.label: Tier 0 — One-Time Transaction (direct spend)
tier_0.description: The project's own one-time transaction: CAPEX paid directly to immediate suppliers in one country.  The investment amount is distributed across 8 supplying sectors according to the project's sector-allocation vector (SECTOR_ALLOC in tvp_io_lib.py) and each sector's spend is multiplied by its regional stressor intensity (S_BASE × REGION_MULT). No Leontief inversion is applied — this is the one-time direct procurement effect.  The SECTOR_ALLOC vector distributes across sectors but conceptually it is still one transaction (the CAPEX investment). Example: for a Rail_Dev project, 35% of CAPEX flows to Construction, 28% to Manufacturing, 10% to Energy_Utilities, etc.
tier_0.math: y₀ = alloc × invest_M$;  impact₀ = S · diag(y₀)

tier_1.label: Tier 1 — First Upstream Round: Tier 0 Spend Aggregated into the Supply Chain
tier_1.description: Tier 1 is not the direct spend — that is Tier 0.  Tier 1 is what happens when the Tier 0 payment propagates one round into the upstream economy.  The construction firms, steel mills, and equipment manufacturers that received the Tier 0 payment now purchase their own inputs from suppliers in different sectors and different countries. One Leontief round: y₁ = A × y₀. Each sector's tier-1 spend is distributed across sourcing countries (Europe, LATAM, Africa, Asia, Global) using bilateral trade shares extracted from the OECD TiVA database (or from the MRIO Z matrix when file-backed databases are loaded). Regional intensity coefficients are applied at the sourcing country, not the project country — so Chinese-sourced steel carries Chinese GHG intensity, not European.
tier_1.math: y₁ = A · y₀;  impact₁[src] = S(src) · diag(trade_share[src] × y₁)

tier_2.label: Tier 2 — Second Upstream Round
tier_2.description: The Tier 1 suppliers' own supply chain — two rounds back from the direct spend.  Computed as y₂ = A² × y₀. Mining_Extraction and Energy_Utilities tend to gain share at tier 2 because most sectors require raw materials and energy as inputs. Note: the sourcing-country breakdown is not computed for tier 2 and above (only aggregate intensities for the project region are applied).
tier_2.math: y₂ = A² · y₀;  impact₂ = S · diag(y₂)

tiers_3_to_10.label: Tiers 3–10 — Deep Upstream Rounds (aggregate + per-tier)
tiers_3_to_10.description: The deeper upstream rounds, computed via successive matrix powers A³ through A¹⁰ applied to y₀. By tier 3–4 the supply chain signal has largely converged: column sums of Aⁿ decay geometrically at roughly the spectral radius of A (≈ 0.52 for the global average A_BASE), so tiers beyond 6 contribute less than 2% of the tier-0 impact. The aggregate block sums impact across tiers 3–10 for each indicator and sector; the per_tier block preserves the tier-by-tier detail for users who need to set a specific truncation point.
tiers_3_to_10.math: yₙ = Aⁿ · y₀  for n ∈ {3,…,10};  impactₙ = S · diag(yₙ)

input_data.notes: CAPEX of €1,850,000,000 converted to USD at EUR/USD = 1.09 (ECB 2023 average), giving $2,016,500,000. Stage 'Dev' maps to sector_code 'Rail_Dev'. This is the main capital investment project (construction phase). Supply chain analysis traces embodied carbon and employment across the procurement of civil works, steel, electrical equipment, and rolling stock.

definition.label: Tier 0 — One-Time Transaction (direct spend)
definition.description: The project's own one-time transaction: CAPEX paid directly to immediate suppliers in one country.  The investment amount is distributed across 8 supplying sectors according to the project's sector-allocation vector (SECTOR_ALLOC in tvp_io_lib.py) and each sector's spend is multiplied by its regional stressor intensity (S_BASE × REGION_MULT). No Leontief inversion is applied — this is the one-time direct procurement effect.  The SECTOR_ALLOC vector distributes across sectors but conceptually it is still one transaction (the CAPEX investment). Example: for a Rail_Dev project, 35% of CAPEX flows to Construction, 28% to Manufacturing, 10% to Energy_Utilities, etc.
definition.math: y₀ = alloc × invest_M$;  impact₀ = S · diag(y₀)

oecd.db_label: OECD ICIO v2021 | 66c | 45 sec | updated | policy/TiVA

definition.label: Tier 1 — First Upstream Round: Tier 0 Spend Aggregated into the Supply Chain
definition.description: Tier 1 is not the direct spend — that is Tier 0.  Tier 1 is what happens when the Tier 0 payment propagates one round into the upstream economy.  The construction firms, steel mills, and equipment manufacturers that received the Tier 0 payment now purchase their own inputs from suppliers in different sectors and different countries. One Leontief round: y₁ = A × y₀. Each sector's tier-1 spend is distributed across sourcing countries (Europe, LATAM, Africa, Asia, Global) using bilateral trade shares extracted from the OECD TiVA database (or from the MRIO Z matrix when file-backed databases are loaded). Regional intensity coefficients are applied at the sourcing country, not the project country — so Chinese-sourced steel carries Chinese GHG intensity, not European.

definition.description: The deeper upstream rounds, computed via successive matrix powers A³ through A¹⁰ applied to y₀. By tier 3–4 the supply chain signal has largely converged: column sums of Aⁿ decay geometrically at roughly the spectral radius of A (≈ 0.52 for the global average A_BASE), so tiers beyond 6 contribute less than 2% of the tier-0 impact. The aggregate block sums impact across tiers 3–10 for each indicator and sector; the per_tier block preserves the tier-by-tier detail for users who need to set a specific truncation point.
definition.math: yₙ = Aⁿ · y₀  for n ∈ {3,…,10};  impactₙ = S · diag(yₙ)

input_data.notes: CAPEX of €130,000 converted to USD at EUR/USD = 1.09 (ECB 2023 average), giving $141,700. Stage 'Op' maps to sector_code 'Rail_Op'. This is an operational-phase expenditure item. The supply chain analysis traces energy, maintenance materials, and service inputs required to run the railway.
